item.title: Did someone call a cryptanalyst?
item.content:encoded:
Saw the code wasnt broken. Looks fun. Will post my findings (if any)

Off the cuff we have only 4 distinct different characters, meaning it can not be something like a polybius square or any cipher that uses the polybius as a base. Maybe Baconian, with the 1/1 being a comma and . being a space? Will definitely dig deeper into this and update this reply if I find anything.

(I'm sorry, I didnt mean to make this a reply to the above comment. Apologies, TorumSie)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, you had me at Cathbhadh and I wanted to give you all the doots by the end of his conversation with the new Administrator. I'm a major fan of a huge number of mythologies and loved watching this unfold.

Using the geode as an anchor was inspired and hints to me of the power of his bindings.

I was confused with how much destruction he was raining down but the conversation really tied it up well for me. He isn't a warmonger, but he will get the job done. He has had to watch too many of his people conquered or enslaved, to many of their civilizations destroyed.

I see his actions as being very controlled, it's more of a culling rather than wanton destruction. Pruning out the elements destined to cause the most impact, in a way guiding the remainder towards a less cohesive and effective organization, if not more moderate.

With his abilities I wouldn't be surprised if each of the deaths in those incidents were carefully selected.

Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this more and more the further I read in it. One quick diversion- I think "ogham letters" would probably be more correct than "oghams." Initially found it weird that a staff memo would've been written by O5-01, but I suppose that makes sense in context.

Oh, uh, not to make this review a bunch of nitpicks because I did really like the concept, but shouldn't the text in that last collapsible be "vivere"? "viventem" is an adjective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Code is completely unbroken. My friend who helped me design it even posted it to a codebreaking subreddit and no one solved it (although it didn't get a ton of attention).

T I'll give this hint: the code has three "layers", and only one of those layers is all that difficult to figure out which is the first one you'd end up solving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, uh…

How we coming on that code breaking? I have theories but no time to test them.

+1, I actually really liked the "This isn't a lie, just do your job" notes followed by "it was a bit of a lie, but now you get why we lied." Followed by "Ok, big cheese, hold onto your pants, here's what's up"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My opinions on this article largely mirror those in their comment, with extra emphasis on the disappointment of the ending. Anomalies trying to act like they're too good for containment is a trope I really dislike as it makes for fairly similar narratives every time, and no part of this made me feel like it was especially justified in this case beyond the anomaly just being quite powerful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made D B Cooper an SCP? Awesome. I have to say i prefer this version over the real one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As an Irish person its always nice to see celtic SCiPs +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ultimately, I felt let down by Cathbhadh's message at the end. "Stop locking up wizards because you're narrow-minded" felt like a really basic, uninteresting conclusion to all the hoops this jumps through on the way to it.

Before that, though, I was disappointed that there wasn't more focus on the oghamstone. I mean, the description is really neat, I wanted to learn more about it, sure they don't understand anything about it, but then the focus shifts entirely to D. B. Cooper.

And that wouldn't have been so bad if the fact that he was D. B. Cooper really mattered. That specific hijacking event doesn't seem to have any effect on the Foundation's ongoing search for this guy; it's just what he's most well known for. And it also bothers me — I went and read the wikipedia article about him in the midst of reading this — that the Foundation doesn't identify him as Dan Cooper first, since that was the guy's real pseudonym. (Unless that's actually meant as a double-blind or something.)

Point is, this has a neat idea at its core, but I just didn't enjoy the way the major parts move around it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure on the usage of Eire for Ireland. It's only been known by that name since 1937, and is officially known as Ireland in English, so that feels a little odd for a being born "[s]ometime before 750 BC".

You might also want to consider Éire (instead of Eire), as eire (no accent) means a burden, and I've know a few Irish object to it on that basic (I've also known many who don't care, and it's sometimes omitted in Irish for uppercase anyway, such as on old stamps or ships).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you made DB Cooper an SCP?

you made him an immortal Celtic druid????

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to Athenodora, snugglyface,Sevatar, rattles,TrusyOlValet2, and IronDruid for their feedback when this was just a draft. This is an (admittedly kind of weird) marriage between my fascination with unsolved crimes and my love of Celtic mythology. I have a running bet with the friend who helped me design the cipher on how long it will take for someone to crack it. There’s some fun historical references in there too.

Image credits: only image was made by me (with help from a friend who wants to remain unamed).

Preferred numbers:
5432
5555
5005
5001
5995
